data.title: Netflix: At The Crossroads Between Expansion And Saturation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome EquityDuo Insights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

With more than 300 million paying subscribers in more than 190 countries, Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) is confirmed year after year as the most influential company in the global entertainment landscape. The platform has a wide and diverse range of audiovisual content, including TV series, movies, and video games, in many different genres and languages.

In the past couple of years, despite already high market penetration, Netflix's attractiveness grew not only due to the quality and amount of content brought to market but also by virtue of a succession of carefully planned strategic decisions that have consolidated the platform and maximized profitability. Two in particular have been responsible: the prohibition on sharing accounts, with the addition of an additional fee for extra members; the introduction of a low-price plan with backing from advertising.

These strategies have enabled Netflix to expand its subscriber base, increase average revenue per user (ARPU), and diversify revenue sources, contributing significantly to the effectiveness of its business model, and this is a testament to the excellent management and foresight of the company.

While recognizing the undoubted strength of the Company, in our view, two critical factors are currently emerging: the performance of the stock and the intensifying competition in the industry. These elements, lead us to believe that investing in Netflix is, at the moment, less attractive than it might appear at first glance.

In this paper, after a brief introduction to the Company and its financial situation, an in-depth analysis of the sector context, the stock's performance, and key indicators, both technical and fundamental, will be conducted.

Netflix Overview

As described earlier, Netflix provides streaming content, but the question remains: how exactly does it work? Content on the platform comes from licensing content from other studios or individual producers, but that isn't the whole story; actually, the Company also produces its own Netflix original content, though this constitutes a tiny fraction of the entire library.

The company's business model is primarily subscription-based. Recently, Netflix has also included advertising as a source of revenue. This serves to lower the initial cost of the basic plan for some subscribers while generating revenue from the ads themselves.

Most notably, its business model relies on three different subscription plans:

The company's strength lies in its resilience; in fact, it seems to continue to find new ways to increase revenue growth and resulting profits while transforming itself into a more mature company. As of December 31, 2024, the majority of Netflix's revenue comes from subscriptions; looking at the Form 10-K, the company had around 300 million subscribers worldwide, generating annual revenues of $39 billion, thus registering a CAGR of about 14% over 2019.

Moving to the valuation of NFLX stock, it can be seen that it is currently trading at about 59 times earnings (actual P/E). Thus, the company is currently trading at a value that the market considers as fair to pay for earnings and revenue growth of this caliber. But is it the fair price to pay? To understand this, we have carried out a careful analysis of both the relevant industry and the company's stock price, aspects that we will look at in the following paragraphs.

Competitors And Main Risks

To date, there is still too little information to accurately predict the growth of the target industry. What can be stated with certainty is the rising saturation of the market, such that each competitor brings a different offering to the table than the others:

Disney (DIS), for example, is enhancing its offering through a model centered on Disney+, focusing on family-friendly content and the strength of its iconic brands, including the great classics. The price is lower than that of Netflix, although the content library is much thinner. Disney recently introduced ESPN to its offering, a product quite distinct from Netflix, which has a smaller footprint in the live sports space. From our point of view, that's a clear competitive advantage of the Mickey Mouse firm over Netflix.

Amazon Prime Video: As we all know, price can be an important factor that many viewers pay attention to. In this case, although the two platforms offer very similar prices for their basic services, we know that Amazon guarantees, with Prime, several additional services such as free shipping and music streaming that go beyond just access to TV and streaming content. Based on the level of editorial offerings, Amazon Prime Video has an overall less extensive library than Netflix; in addition, access to certain content requires separate purchase or rental, as it is not included in the standard subscription. In contrast, Netflix maintains a competitive advantage due to the wide availability of original content and the relative full accessibility of the same.

HBO: Although HBO Max's library is less extensive than that of other competitors, the platform stands out for its high-quality content offerings, particularly through HBO Originals content and Warner Bros. films. One of HBO's main strengths is the availability, even a few months after theatrical release, of Warner Bros films directly on the platform, a competitive advantage over Netflix, where exclusive titles from the majors often require longer wait times. Finally, HBO, as well as Disney or Prime Video itself, offers live sports content through the B/R Sports Add-On, which includes NBA, MLB, and NHL events, thus expanding its offerings beyond traditional entertainment.

What this qualitative analysis finds is that each of the competitors under study, and a good number of others, differ in what they provide, and the choice will be based on both consumers' personal preferences regarding content viewing and their budget. In this competitive environment, in our opinion, if Netflix fails to attract new customers, the main alternatives to grow its business volume will be either increased subscription prices or an increase in the advertising rate.

An additional element of risk for Netflix lies in its current market valuation. As anticipated, the stock currently trades at multiples around 59x earnings. To date, these levels have been supported by strong growth and margin expansion in recent years. However, as is often the case, should the market begin to perceive Netflix as a company with more moderate growth, the stock could begin to trade on reduced P/E multiples, in our view, as low as half the current levels.

In order to better understand the possible price trajectories of the stock, we decided to devote the next section to technical analysis and the study of key momentum and trend indicators.

Technical Analysis

We start the technical analysis by looking at the NFLX ticker chart, on a candlestick chart in the daily timeframe (Daily:D).

We considered 3 main dimensions of analysis: price analysis, volume analysis, and indicator analysis (in particular, as “bounded oscillator,” we used the RSI, and as “center-line momentum indicators," we used the MACD and TRIX to confirm).

Looking at the price structure, the primary movement remains bullish, with a significant dynamic support (positively inclined red line) that went from resistance to support in early May. However, the last swing from 25 April 2025 to today developed within an "upward widening wedge" (the two thinnest purple trendlines diverging). This is a bearish breakout pattern if broken down.

As regards volumes, there has been a steady decline in the last two months, which is in divergence from the movement of prices. This is in fact the typical price-volume divergence that precedes the "exhaustion of a trend"; to assess a breakout, we will wait for volumes to return at least above the 20-period moving average.

Let us now focus on the technical indicators: looking at the MACD chart, the red line (signal) has just crossed the blue line [MACD] from bottom to top, forming a "dead cross", signaling a possible bearish trend. The divergence between MACD and prices is also evident. We also notice how the histogram approaches zero, signaling a loss of momentum. The TRIX (18) (triple exponential moving average) shows a downward trajectory of 35 bars. The divergence price-indicator, again confirms the bearish bias.

As for RSI (14), we note the loss of momentum after reaching overbought last week in June; the key threshold we monitor is 40.

In summary, the graphic conformation, combined with the various divergences visible both on the volumes and on the indicators, confirms an asymmetrically bearish setup. We found our target by projecting the Fibonacci retracement from the relative low of Monday, April 7, 2025 to the relative high of Monday, June 30, 2025. The level we take as our target is $1,019.72, at the “0.618” level of the Fibonacci retracement.

To validate the findings from the technical analysis, we conducted a valuation using the DCF method, presented in the following paragraph.

Valuation

The Discounted Cash Flow [DCF] method was adopted to determine the fair value of Netflix. The DCF was constructed based on a number of assumptions, summarized below and developed by referring to historical data, comparative analysis, and guidance provided by the company itself.

First, revenue growth of 13 percent was assumed for 2025, in line with the company's guidance. This increase would bring revenues to between $44.0 billion and $45.0 billion. For subsequent years, on the other hand, revenues were projected using the average growth rate recorded over the past three fiscal years, i.e., from 2021 to the present.

As for the cost structure, COGS and other operating costs, such as general and administrative expenses (SG&A), were estimated based on historical incidences relative to revenues. The same logic was also applied for estimating changes in NWC and Capex.

An explicit projection time horizon of 5 years (2025 to 2030) was assumed for the valuation model. At the end of this period, the Terminal Value was estimated, assuming that the Company enters a stable and sustainable long-term growth phase. For the purpose of terminal value calculation, a terminal (perpetuity) growth rate of 3% was adopted. This choice reflects the Company's optimistic view of its long-term prospects, also in light of management statements, which aim to reach a market capitalization of one trillion dollars by 2030.

The WACC was estimated by taking into account the weighted average cost of financing sources, equity and debt. Specifically, the cost of equity was determined through the CAPM, using a Risk-free rate of 4.26% related to the US 10Y Bond, an equity risk premium of 4.21% based on the historical ERP by Damodaran; a sector average unlevered beta of 0.60; and finally, an illiquidity size premium of 1.25% related to Companies with large market capitalization. The cost of debt, on the other hand, was estimated from the same Risk free rate, to which was added a spread on debt of 5.4%, A-rated Yield Curve; and a tax rate of 25.6%. Based on what has been described, the selected WACC is equal to 8.17%.

It should be noted that for the estimation of some of the above components, and to be precise, beta unlevered and D/E (equal to 0.17), a comparative analysis was conducted with some of the main competitors in the industry, including Disney, Warner Bros (WBD), Roku and Paramount, through which it was possible to determine average industry values.

In light of these assumptions, the results derived from the model are summarized in the table below.

As can be seen from the table, the present value of operating cash flows generated over the plan period is $83 billion, while the terminal value [TV], discounted to the valuation date, is approximately $235 billion. The sum of these two components yields an enterprise value (EV) of about $318 billion.

Subtracting net financial debt and other non-operating items results in an equity value (EQV) of about $310 billion. Dividing the EQV by the number of shares outstanding, which is approximately 427 million shares, yields a theoretical price per share of $726.

Disclaimer: Since the DCF method is highly sensitive to changes in the WACC and terminal growth-rate parameters, a sensitivity analysis was performed on these quantities to highlight the impact that small changes in rates can have on the final market value.

Investment Thesis Upside Drivers and Risk Factors

As is well known, Netflix released its second quarter results on July 17. The report positively confirmed analysts' expectations, but in our opinion still leaves room for reflection, especially for those considering an entry into the stock at current price levels.

The company reported a solid quarter, with revenue at $11.08 billion, slightly above estimates of $11.04 billion, and earnings per share [EPS] also above expectations at about $7.19 versus $7.07 estimated. Netflix also revised upward its guidance for 2025, forecasting revenues in the range of $44.8 billion to $45.2 billion, attributing this growth to increased subscribers and stronger revenues from the advertising business.

That said, although 2Q slightly beat expectations, our optimistic valuation through the DCF model, highlights how the current share price is discounting a growth scenario that is difficult to sustain in the coming years, especially in an environment of increasing competition.

In the short to medium term, the stock's performance will depend on how the market and analysts interpret this data. If sentiment is positive, then in all likelihood our thesis may be challenged. Nevertheless, we maintain our first bearish target at $1020, believing that much of the upside is already discounted in current prices.

Moving to a longer-term perspective, we emphasize how the introduction of advertising in the basic plan has enabled Netflix to reach an even larger customer base than it already possessed.

In any case, the company's performance in the coming months and years will depend on Netflix’s ability to continue to attract new subscribers or, alternatively, to raise the average price of its subscriptions. What is certain is that a surprise in the number of subscribers may act as a catalyst pushing the stock to new highs.

Conclusion

For years now, Netflix has shown strength and strategic vision, however, its current market value and intensifying competition raise real uncertainties about the possible future growth of the stock. The real risk to date is not about how Netflix is positioned today, but what kind of growth the market is pricing in. Our view is that this future is still too optimistic, for this reason, our assessment of the stock is clear: SELL Netflix.

data.title: Pharvaris: More Convenience, More Potency, And More Potential For Future HAE Success
data.description:
Thesis

Pharvaris (NASDAQ:PHVS) will release topline results for its Phase 3 trial, Rapide-3, for immediate-release deucrictibant in Q4 2025. Analysis of their clinical trial data and the medical literature leads me to believe they have a fairly good chance of succeeding in getting FDA approval, which will add a bullish momentum for the stock, in my view.

Overview of Pharvaris’ Pipeline

The entire pipeline consists of only one drug: deucrictibant (a.k.a. PHA-022121 or PHA-121). Pharvaris is developing deucrictibant as two oral formulations:

1) Immediate-release capsule for acute attacks. This is also called “On Demand Treatment” (ODT).

2) Extended-release (“XR”) tablets for long-term prophylactic treatment.

Deucrictibant’s mechanism of action is as a bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist. This makes it the first oral B2 receptor antagonist. The only other established drug that has the same mechanism is icatibant, which has been around for 15 years in injection form. More on the science later.

A Quick Look at Pharvaris’ Financials

As of December 31, 2024, Pharvaris had €280(~$325) million in cash. They have a yearly burn rate of around €115 million in cash (taking the average of the last 2 years as these were closer to later phase trials). Given that we’re halfway through the year, they’ve most likely already gone through around €70 million, leaving them with ~€200 million in cash. All this to say that they are burning through cash quite fast.

Valuation

The current market for HAE treatment stands at about $3.365 billion per year and is projected to grow to ~$6 billion by the 2030s. The largest share of this market is held by Takeda Pharmaceuticals' (TAK) TAKHZYRO (lanadelumab), which is a monoclonal antibody targeted against plasma kallikrein. The generic icatibant market + branded icatibant (FIRAZYR, also Takeda) comes to around $300 million worth of revenue a year.

The oral drugs that are currently on the market are ones that attack plasma kallikrein for their mechanisms of action. These are: BioCryst’s Orladeyo, and KalVista's Ekterly (sebetrelstat) which was approved very recently.

I will now make a basic but very conservative (a value investor’s dream) forecast for deucrictibant with the information we have. Assumptions for the forecast are as follows:

1) Deucrictibant will only ever reach a maximum revenue of $350 million, even if the projected market will reach $6 billion by the 2030s. This is ~10% of the market share, as of today.

2) It will take 3 years to reach maximum market penetration of $350 million, and it will stay at the max until the expiry of the exclusivity patent.

3) The exclusivity patent will last 7 years (just like icatibant).

4) Once the exclusivity ends, revenue will immediately fall to 0.

5) A discount rate of 6% for DCF/NPV

6) The final assumption is, of course, that deucrictibant will pass Phase 3 for all of this to happen.

Thus, a basic cash flow with these assumptions might look like this (in millions):

Year 1st 2nd 3rd 4th 5th 6th 7th 8th
Revenue $70 $175 $350 $350 $350 $350 $350 $0
Discount 1.06 1.06^2 1.06^3 1.06^4 1.06^5 1.06^6 1.06^7 1.06^8

With a discount rate of 6%, we get an NPV of $1.533 billion after discounting all future cash flows and adding them all up. And with 54.5 million shares outstanding, we get a share value of 1.533/0.054 = $28.39. The current price (which jumped up quite a fair bit after Pharvaris’ announcement of their topline results arriving in Q4) is $23 (as of 16 July). This gives us a potential upside of around 23.5% for the stock.

Now, as I said before, I am being very conservative (brutal even), but on the flip side, I’m automatically assuming the drug will succeed in getting approval, which I do believe in.

Risks to this thesis

Naturally, Deucrictibant failing its phase 3 trial is the biggest risk. I find this unlikely, as I explain in the science section below. The second risk is that being a small-cap biopharma stock with only one drug in its pipeline makes Pharvaris very susceptible to investor sentiment and risk flows. For instance, any talk of further trade wars by Trump could easily hurt the price (on the bright side, that indicates a better buying opportunity if you believe in the stock). A third risk that some might think of is the recent FDA approval of KalVista’s Ekterly, which could take away market share from Deucrictibant. However, I believe Ekterly won’t compromise deucrictibant’s share as it targets a different mechanism and instead will take market share away from the already established injectable formulations for that specific mechanism. If anything, Deucrictibant has a higher chance of biting into the non-B2 receptor mechanism share of the market since it makes up such a large portion of the current market.

With the financial analysis out of the way, let us proceed to break down the medical science behind Deucrictibant.

Increased Potency and Convenience of Deucrictibant

It’s no secret that Pharvaris is basing their bet on Deucrictibant off the fact that Icatibant has shown success for the last 15 years since Deucrictibant attacks the same target as Icatibant. That is, Pharvaris isn’t trying to reinvent the wheel but rather maximize convenience. After all, even those who aren’t scared of needles will admit at some point that it would be much easier to just pop a pill (especially if the first injection doesn’t work and you would have to reinject later again or if one is in public and doesn’t want to get weird looks). The convenience factor must not be underestimated. It’s a pretty big deal for patients, and it’s about time.

Deucrictibant blocks the same B2 receptor as icatibant. However, it is a different, smaller molecule and one that is more potent than icatibant. Potency in pharmacological terms doesn’t mean it is stronger. That is, using arbitrary units and doses as an example, it doesn’t mean if you were to take X mg of deucrictibant, you would get 200 units of clinical effect, and if you were to take X mg of icatibant, you would only get 100 units of clinical effect. Potency simply means Deucrictibant needs a lower concentration (drug dissolved in a certain amount of blood) to get the same 100 units of clinical effect compared to Icatibant.

The absolute max clinical effect of a drug is called “Efficacy”. While there isn’t a direct comparison for the efficacy of deucrictibant vs. icatibant, they are likely to have similar efficacies with a slight edge to deucrictibant, as icatibant has been shown to have partial agonist activity at high doses while, according to Pharvaris, deucrictibant doesn’t.

The tables below demonstrate potency comparisons

The potencies range from the mid-20s (using Schild Analysis, which is basically where you give the antagonist and check how much of the agonist you have to add to activate the receptor once again) all the way to ~4x for direct comparisons between “effective concentrations” (EC50, EC85). The ECs are basically a percentage of the maximum clinical effect. So, when I used the arbitrary clinical effect units of 100 in the example above, EC85 would equate to 85 units of clinical effect. In the real-world scenario of deucrictibant, the clinical effect is normalizing bradykinin-challenge-induced symptoms such as changes in blood pressure, cutaneous blood flow, and heart rate. For example, if a bradykinin-challenge (HAE attack) increased heart rate by 20 beats per minute and a 100% effect (a.k.a. Emax) of Deucrictibant was to normalize it back by 20 beats per minute, then EC85 would be the effective concentration at which it normalized by 20 * 0.85 = 17 bpm (I’m simplifying with arbitrary numbers).

Long story short, Deucrictibant in the worst-case scenario is 4x as potent (you need 4x less concentration to have an equal effect) as Icatibant. This makes sense as when you take drugs orally, a higher percentage of the drug gets eliminated by the liver (lower first-pass bioavailability) as opposed to injecting subcutaneously, so having a more potent drug protects against loss of the drug in the liver.

Why is deucrictibant more potent than icatibant? In the absence of efficacy reasons, the explanation that remains is because deucrictibant stays attached to the receptor longer (a weaker dissociation constant). This effect becomes evident in the duration of the clinical effect.

The duration of effect shown in PD (effect), where blue = deucrictibant taken orally and red = icatibant, which was injected twice subcutaneously with a 6-hour interval between each dose. For the 22-mg dose, Deucrictibant stays a fair amount of time above EC85 relative to a single dose of Icatibant and even longer above EC50 for double doses of Icatibant. Granted, this was a simulation, but real pharmacokinetic models from patients showed even slightly longer durations above EC85.

PGI-C tracks well with VAS-3

The clinical endpoint for the Rapide-3 trial for immediate-release Deucricitbant is the end of progression for a patient's symptoms, which is tracked with the Patient Global Impression of Change (PGI-C) being equal to “a little better.”.

Icatibant in its Phase 3 trial (FAST-3) had utilized median time to 50% improvement in composite VAS-3 as a clinical endpoint. VAS-3 stands for Visual Analog Scale-3, which is a visual way of asking the patient on a scale of 1 to 10 how bad the symptom is, and this is done for 3 symptoms (abdominal pain, skin swelling, and skin pain). It’s not the most objective way of measuring a clinical effect, which makes it susceptible to variability between patients, but a lot of the treatment in HAE is based on patient perception. Icatibant achieved a median time of 2 (with a 95% CI of 1.5 to 3) hours until 50% improvement compared to Placebo achieving 19 hours.

Deucrictibant in its Phase 2 trial (Rapide-1) also utilized VAS-3, albeit with a slightly different clinical endpoint. The measure was how much the patient had improved by the 4-hour mark. The graph below is the result.

This was a fairly well-powered study, so these results aren’t too statistically random (with the exception of VAS-3 itself being inherently variable due to subjectivity).

AMRA-3 is basically VAS-3, just a non-visual numeric version of it. A study was conducted, not by Pharvaris and having nothing to do with deucrictibant, in the USA to see how AMRA-3 matches to other patient reported outcomes (PROs) such as PGI-C and PGI-S (severity). The main drugs used were icatibant (60/90) and plasma-derived C1-INH (22/90). The graph below shows the relationships between them.

Generally, they match quite well. The more impressive improvements (PGI-C “better,” AMRA-3 50%, and PGI-S 1-level) tracked each other better as 1 cluster, and the slight improvements tracked each other better in their own cluster.

And this is the actual results for PGI-C “a little better," which is the equivalent of “onset of symptom relief," for Deucrictibant (multiple dose groups combined into 1) in the Rapide-2 (open-label extension study of Rapide-1 but no placebo group control anymore).

The only thing I wish they did was to have used PGI-C from the very beginning of their Rapide-1 study, and this way we would have been able to compare to Placebo because, again, it’s a bit difficult to gauge with subjective patient-reported outcomes.

Once again, to recap, deucrictibant did well in VAS-3, which is the same as AMRA-3, which has a similar tracking relationship with PGI-C “a little better,” which means results on VAS-3 have a high likelihood to translate into meaningful results in PGI-C “a little better.”

What about long-term prophylactic treatment?

A lot of the logic here that went into showing that immediate-release deucrictibant does indeed act in a considerable way on the receptor target (similarly to icatibant) will also support its success as a long-term prophylactic treatment. The only main difference is that the drug has to be formulated to keep concentration above an important level for a much longer time. Pharvaris claims to have accomplished this with their extended-release (XR) tablets. The graph below compares the 2 formulations.

And we can see that the PHVS719 (which is XR tablets) stays above EC85 for up to almost 36 hours.

Another very interesting graph to note is from the open-label extension study for the Chapter 1 trial

We can see that the moment the placebo transfers into the 40mg/day group at the end of the 3-month mark, it immediately reduces the attack rate significantly within the same month.

I have genuine hopes for the XR tablets, as the current king of the market in prophylactic treatment is TAKHZYRO, which is a mAb, and those are expensive. Not only would XR deucrictibant be more convenient for patients, but it would most likely be cheaper for patients as well as insurance companies, governments, etc., thus really giving it the potential to succeed.

Considering all that has been said above, I am bullish Pharvaris stock.

data.title: Teledyne Technologies: Baked-In Strong Military And Drone Tailwinds
data.description:
Teledyne Technologies (NYSE:TDY) is a diversified provider of imaging, sensing, and electronic control systems for the global industrial, defense, and aerospace markets. The company's key Digital Imaging Segment (54% of sales in FY24) struggled early last year but returned to growth in Q4 and is expected to rebound strongly this year with a recovery in the industrial automation and commercial infrared systems markets. Meantime, European defense budgets are on the rise and the company's FLIR Defense business should see strong growth with its Black Hornet, SkyRaider, and Rogue 1 drone solutions. Today, I'll take a look at Teledyne to see if it might be a good way to play the defense sector's transition toward cheaper, more effective, and more strategic drone technology.

Investment Thesis

The graphic below was taken from Teledyne Technologies' 2024 Annual Report and depicts TDY's revenues broken down into its four operating segments:

However, note that there is a relatively high-degree of cross-pollination going on between these segments. That is, the Aerospace & Defense segment no doubt uses technology developed in the Digital Imaging Segment while the Engineering Systems Segment very likely uses products from the Instrumentation Segment, etc. My point is that Teledyne is a company built on a very strong base of technological innovation and solutions.

That said, TDY stock has been a relative laggard over the past 5-years as compared to the broader industrial and defense sectors as represented by the SPDR Industrial Sector ETF (XLI), the iShares US Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), and the broad-market Vanguard S&P500 ETF (VOO):

Part of the reason for TDY's under-performance may be due to the relative stagnation of revenue growth (see graphic below from the company's June Presentation) following the company's $8 billion cash-and-stock deal to acquire FLIR Systems in 2021. FLIR was known for its thermal imaging cameras and intelligent sensing solutions. However, the acquisition was likely over-shadowed by the impacts of Boeing's (BA) troubles and the overhang it cast over practically the entire aerospace supply-chain.

As you can see from the graphic, TDY's revenue growth from 2022-2024 was not very impressive. However, sometimes laggards can regain their strength and outperform going forward and Teledyne's revenue is expected to grow +5.8% yoy in FY25. So, let's take a closer look to see if Teledyne might be a good choice for your portfolio.

Earnings

Teledyne released its Q1 Earnings Report in April and here are some of the highlights:

  • Q1 revenue of $1.45 billion was a record and +7.4% yoy.
  • EPS of $3.99 was +5.7% yoy.
  • Operating margin of 17.9% was up 50 basis points yoy.
  • During the quarter, Teledyne completed two acquisitions (Qioptiq & Micropac) for a combined total of $757.6 million.
  • Free-cash-flow was $224.6 million.

FCF per share, based on 47.3 million average shares, was $4.75. The company ended the quarter with $461.5 million in cash and net-debt of ~$2 billion.

Teledyne Executive Chairman Robert Mehrabian commented on the first quarter's results:

First quarter sales reflected organic growth in every segment, coupled with the contribution from recent acquisitions. In addition, quarter-end backlog was an all-time record, as orders exceeded sales for the sixth consecutive quarter. We continue to execute our strategy, which has delivered long-term results regardless of economic and political uncertainty.

The Aerospace & Defense Electronics Segment was arguably the star of the show, with the segment's Q1 sales of $242.5 million, compared with $185.7 million, or an increase of 30.6% yoy. The increase was primarily due to higher sales of defense electronics and contributions of $42.3 million of incremental defense electronics sales from recent acquisitions.

Going Forward

Teledyne is well-positioned to benefit from increased defense spending in Europe. In 2024, the company had almost $500 million in sales to Europe and has manufacturing plants in the UK, Sweden, Denmark, and Iceland. On the Q1 conference call, Mehrabian said:

European defense budget at about $500 billion, have been about 2% of the GDP. Everything we hear says they're planning to increase that to $800 billion to $900 billion over the next five years. As a minimum, we expect to maintain our share with what we have, which is the $447 million growing proportionate to what they increase their budgets with as. Furthermore, we think considering the kinds of products that we make, we're going to enjoy getting more of the share of those primarily due to the fact that we have unique products that they need, like our unmanned systems."

Speaking of unmanned systems, Teledyne's FLIR Defense issued a press release following U.S. Defense Secretary's "Drone Dominance Directive" of July 10. Dr. JihFen Lei, president of Teledyne FLIR Defense, said:

Teledyne FLIR Defense readily supports U.S. Department of Defense efforts to bolster our drone readiness and ensure all our warfighters are equipped with the right drone systems, technology, and training to achieve success on the battlefield. The Black Hornet nano-drone, shown flying in Secretary Hegseth’s video, has been supporting America’s military for more than six years.

FLIR Defense has delivered more than 33,000 Black Hornet drones to over 45 countries. The newest generation of the company's light-weight nano-drones is the Black Hornet 4, which is outfitted with a 12-megapixel daytime camera and high-resolution thermal imaging to provide enhanced covert surveillance and crisp video feeds for operators directing quick acting small fighting units:

The Black Hornet 4 weighs just 70 grams and can fly for more than 30 minutes, over three kilometers, and can function in 25-knot winds and rain. As a result of this and other products, FLIR appears very well positioned to expand Teledyne's drone business in Europe (and beyond). Indeed, in February of last year FLIR announced it would deliver more than 800 drones to the Canadian government, worth CAD $95 million, for Ukraine.

Note that GE Aerospace (GE) released very strong Q2 results today (Thursday, July 17th), with revenue +21% yoy. The company raised its FY 2025 guidance and its 2028 outlook. I think the read across bodes well for Teledyne Technologies going forward.

In its Q1 earnings release, Teledyne management guided for Q2 GAAP earnings to be in the range of $4.00-$5.15 and for full-year earnings to be in the range of $17.35-$17.83. The mid-point of FY25 guidance ($17.59) would be +2.2% as compared to the $17.21/share earned last year. Placing a 25x multiple (roughly in-line with TDY's 3-year average) suggests a stock price of $440. At pixel time, TDY stock is currently trading at $546.63 and is +36% over the past 12-months.

Risks

Teledyne Technologies faces most all of the same macro-economic risks as most other companies do these days: tariffs and tariff uncertainty, and the potential for elevated cost inflation. However, TDY's diversified global manufacturing base can potentially help mitigate the impact of tariffs.

Despite TDY's recent and very active M&A, as of the end of Q1 the company had a solid balance sheet, with net-debt/EBITDA of ~1.8x. Combined with a strong free-cash-flow profile and a ~$4 billion backlog, the company should be able to continue with strategic acquisitions and/or share buybacks.

As alluded to earlier, the biggest risk for TDY shareholders may well be the stock's recent strong performance. The TTM P/E=31.3x and the forward P/E=30.2x. Despite my bullish outlook on TDY's potential moving forward, that is arguably a relatively rich valuation for a company that - if it meets the midpoint of management's EPS guidance - is only going to grow earnings by 2.2% this year.

Summary & Conclusion

I like Teledyne's strong technology base, its position in nano-drones, and the company's potential to benefit from a recovering commercial aerospace industry and the growth outlook in military sales to Europe. That said, much of TDY's potential already seems to be baked into the stock, which I rate as a HOLD.

Instead, investors might take a look at what I consider to be a much better defense/drone opportunity with the SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR). As I explained in my Seeking Alpha article XAR: The Best Offense Is A Good Defense, this ETF employs an equal-weight strategy. Such a strategy means XAR is more highly levered to the smaller and faster-growing defense companies (i.e., drone developers) as compared to the slower-moving defense Goliath's that have dominated the defense & aerospace sector for decades.

I'll end with a 3-year total returns comparison of TDY with the XAR ETF:

As you can see from the graphic, the XAR ETF has significantly outperformed Teledyne Technologies over the past 3 years, and I see no reason why this trend will not continue over the next few years.

data.title: Inhibrx Biosciences: Seeing Soon If The Momentum Is Just Hype
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

When I first covered Inhibrx Biosciences (NASDAQ:INBX) back in November 2024, I was impressed by their move into late-stage clinical trials, and overall came away with a pretty optimistic outlook on the company, relative to where it sat in terms of overall valuation (around $225 million back then). Since early June, we've seen a surge in share price appreciation, and now I want to take another look at this company, deep into its efforts post-reincorporation. Is there anything to bite on now?

Pipeline Updates

Ozekibart (formerly INBRX-109)

The main project in development for INBX continues to be the DR5 agonist ozekibart, intended to exploit its suppression that cancer uses to prevent cell death. If you want to read more, I covered some of the background of this agent in my previous article.

Importantly, we've seen some preliminary work previously coming out of phase 1 studies in soft tissue sarcoma and chondrosarcoma. The latter tumor type is currently being evaluated in a phase 2 trial with registrational intent. Data from this study are anticipated in Q3 2025. Their ongoing expansion cohort study in patients with Ewing sarcoma is expected to read out sometime in the second half of 2025, as well. Beyond these operational updates, the company has been mum on the sarcoma stories.

However, we did get a preliminary readout of ozekibart safety and efficacy from a phase 1 clinical trial presented at the ASCO GI meeting back in January. Adding ozekibart to standard FOLFIRI doublet chemotherapy for colorectal cancer (CRC) was shown to be feasible. Out of 10 efficacy-assessable patients who received ozekibart, 4 achieved an objective response to treatment, and median PFS was 7.85 months. All patients were previously treated, and response rates tend to drop off a cliff in metastatic CRC after first-line therapy.

This has prompted INBX to initiate an expansion cohort enrolling up to 50 patients with 2 or 3 prior lines of systemic therapy, and the company hopes to share findings from this study in Q3 2025.

INBRX-106

The OX40-targeted molecule INBRX-106 is INBX's other pipeline contender, with a particular focus in head and neck and non-small cell lung cancers. I covered the data we know to date in the previous article, and the only updates we've gotten in 2025 have been guidance on ongoing trial readouts.

Specifically, INBX intends to announce initial phase 2 data of INBRX-106 in combination with pembrolizumab in Q4 2025. They will also divulge interim phase 1/2 data in relapsed or refractory non-small cell lung cancer in Q4.

Financial Overview

As of their latest quarterly report, INBX held $216.5 million in cash and equivalents. Operating expenses were $42.9 million, and after interest, the net loss for the quarter was $43.3 million. The negative cash flow was $35.9 million, implying a cash runway of around 6 quarters. This suggests that, if costs are contained at current levels, that INBX has funding sufficient to around Q3 2026.

Strengths and Risks, Revisited

Strength - Funding sufficient to get to critical data readouts in 2025

Through a loan of $100 million, INBX has shorn up its finances for the near term, continuing to allow them to move forward toward some key upcoming catalysts guided in their most recent communications. Specifically, Q3 and Q4 appear to be the make-or-break times for the company, as a number of key data readouts are expected in sarcoma, head and neck cancer, and lung cancer.

They will not yet be quite in a cash crisis at that point, but I expect they're hoping to leverage these data readouts into a substantial equity raise from a relative position of strength. So, in my view, dilution is coming one way or another.

Risk - The most advanced project is in an area with a lot of failures

DR5 remains a highly risky target marred by failure over the years. Nothing we've seen since my last article covering the company has really changed that perspective, in my view. I will say that the colorectal cancer data give an enticing signal of activity, though, and if they can validate these high response rates in further study, that could be an important avenue forward.

However, overall the DR5 and OX40 stories remain very much in the proving stage. What INBX has going for it now is a boatload of anticipated data readouts coming in the back half of the year. Any one of these studies could be an important catalyst. But they are far from guaranteed to move the needle, as well.

Bottom-Line Summary

I feel one could make a decent case for buying into the market's hype that's been building for months now. There are a lot of catalysts being guided by INBX. However, it is important to temper expectations, as well. For the most part, these are likely to be relatively limited in impact, since we're talking about updates to phase 1 trials.

Of course, the chondrosarcoma readout could open the door to an approval, given that this is a rare disease state that has a high unmet need. But the market's current run on the stock has me wondering how much "sell the news" will occur once these readouts are done. And beyond that, INBX will need to raise funds, and I imagine they'll seek to do so on the back of these data.

Overall, my sentiment at these price levels, after this much share price appreciation, is neutral. I am excited for the prospect of data, but I am not excited for the wait that will come after, and the likely dilutive financing. This one is well worth watching, and if you're particularly risk tolerant, potentially a small position. However, I am content to sit pat and observe here.

data.title: Bond Market Outlook: Resilience And Rate Cuts
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head of North America Investment Grade Credit | Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

June was another positive month for bonds. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index was up about 1.5% and the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index up around 1.8%; both indexes are up just over 4% year-to-date.1 We discuss these returns, Federal Reserve (Fed) moves, and the economy in our monthly question and answer.

Craig: What do you think has been the catalyst for bond returns?

Matt: It’s been a better year than many people probably expected given the noise around tariffs and inflation. For me, it’s that the economy has been resilient. Inflation hasn’t been as bad as people had feared, and while the tariffs have been in the headlines, they haven’t been as punitive as people thought from an inflationary perspective. That may change, however, as we see more data. Overall, we think that means that the Fed is likely to cut rates at some point this year. That has led some market participants to front-run potential cuts with an increased risk appetite.

Craig: The market believes we’ll see cuts in September and December. Do you agree with that, particularly if inflation remains stickier? Or do you think the market is being a bit aggressive on its expectations for cuts?

Matt: Looking at the economic data, particularly around employment, it’s slowing. Although, we recently had some decent job openings and payroll numbers. So that’s good. But overall, we’re seeing the economy slow. The Fed is currently in a restrictive stance, which means you’re going to make the economy slow even more, which I don’t think they want. So even though it’s been resilient, I’m concerned that if the Fed doesn’t cut sooner, they may be behind the curve. I think they should cut in July; however, I don’t think they will. I think Fed Chairman Jerome Powell will take a cut instead and then explain his forward-looking views on rates at the Jackson Hole Economic Symposium in August. We think the Fed will likely cut in September, and then if they’re behind the curve, you could potentially see two more cuts by the end of the year. The sooner they move, ironically, the less they may have to cut overall.

Craig: Let’s talk about credit spreads. The S&P 500 ended June at new highs, and July has historically been a strong month too. Why are credit spreads struggling to keep up in this market?

Matt: It’s been a bit frustrating to see credit spreads go sideways to slightly tighter in June while stocks just went up and up. Our view is that the economy would hang in well enough and that eventually many of the negative headlines would go away. That would allow markets to go higher, which usually means credit spreads can go tighter. For now, it hasn’t turned out that way. I think there are two reasons why. First, spreads are already tight, making it harder for them to move tighter. Second, yields overall have come down. We’ve had 5%-5.50% yields on the investment grade index all year.2 As we got higher on that range, there have been more buyers, and as we got lower on that range, we’ve seen a bit more cost consciousness from investors. They’ve been a little bit more particular to the lower end of yield range.

So, right now, institutional investors have pulled back just a touch, but I think we’re still missing the individual investors, who have been waiting to enter the bond market. I think that with a more than 4% total return year-to-date,3 we may see them start moving off the sidelines. It may be because of a bit of FOMO (fear of missing out), where they want to start participating before yields are materially lower.

Craig: The market almost seems like it’s on summer break right now. Stocks are at all-time highs and credit spreads at all-time tights. Do you think there has been a little complacency in this type of environment?

Matt: There’s probably some complacency, but overall, I think there’s a lot of good that doesn’t always get reported. I continue to think that the economy has been on great footing. Fundamentals have been good from a balance sheet standpoint, so there’s really no reason to panic. I think spreads may go tighter from here.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P. The year-to-date returns as of June 30, 2025, were 4.02% for the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index and 4.17% for the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index.

  2. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Bloomberg US Credit Index as of June 30, 2025.

  3. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Bloomberg US Aggregate Index return as of June 30, 2025.

Important information

NA4660953

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment grade, fixed-rate bond market.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes US dollar-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

The yield curve plots interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates to project future interest rate changes and economic activity.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of July 17, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Bond market outlook: Resilience and rate cuts by Invesco US

data.title: Rates Spark: Next Week Should Be More Eventful For Euro Rates
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

Data surprises more likely a driver next week than ECB surprises

For euro rates, this week turned out to be quite uneventful, but next week should bring more for markets to work with. For one, we have the ECB meeting on July 24. Although we don’t see a cut, we will be listening for hints about the path forward. So far, the ECB has carefully staged each cut, with no surprises on the day of the actual meetings. Markets do see another 25bp cut, but have not yet decided on the September or December meeting. This means the ECB will have some work to do if it wants to orchestrate its next move. Having said that, this will hinge on Trump’s eventual tariff levels and their impact, something we - and the ECB - can only speculate about for the time being.

Whereas the front end of the curve will likely see little movement, the drift higher in longer-dated yields could continue if we see a good set of PMIs on Thursday. The Citi economic surprise index for the eurozone is now at its highest since April 2024, reflecting better-than-expected readings over the past three months. Of course, the consensus has also been more downbeat since "Liberation Day", lowering the bar for upside data surprises. But still, a series of good economic numbers could accelerate the rise in the 10Y swap rate. Economic headwinds, including the aforementioned tariff threats, are keeping rates lower, but structural forces like quantitative tightening and EU spending ambitions open the path to higher levels.

Meanwhile, Japanese government bonds (JGBs) continue to be under pressure against a backdrop of fiscal concerns and inflation risks, with higher long-end rates increasingly spilling over to global rate markets. With the Bank of Japan in hiking mode and the ECB still cutting, European government bonds are looking increasingly attractive on an FX-hedged basis for Japanese institutional investors. The upward yield pressure can, therefore, continue if domestic investors seek foreign alternatives for their JGB holdings.

Friday’s events and market views

Little data is expected. The US will publish building permits and housing starts, which gauge the direction of the housing market. We also have the Michigan consumer sentiment index for July, which consensus sees as broadly stable.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: At What Price Would I Buy Energy Transfer?
data.description:
I last wrote about Energy Transfer LP Common Units (ET) back in 2012, and it has provided total returns of about 350% since my last article, and I have sold my position. It's been a very good run and it seems like Energy Transfer has become increasingly popular for income investors. However, I also feel that some complacency has also arrived with this increased popularity, and that could mean some key risks are being glossed over. Let's take a look below at some of the pros and cons when it comes to investing in Energy Transfer.

The Chart

As shown below, Energy Transfer is trading just below the 50-day and 200-day moving average, both of which are right around $17.70. Right around the end of May, a bearish death cross formed on the chart, although only slightly so. With the 50-day and 200-day moving averages almost exactly at the same level now, it looks like this could go either way. I do want to point out that Energy Transfer was trading in the $15 range for a good part of 2024, and it again dropped even to the $14 range in April, and traded for about $15, in May. I feel that this tends to indicate there is some "gravitational pull" and a pattern of selloffs to the $14 to $15 range, and I believe there will be more pullbacks to this level in the months ahead.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The earnings estimates (as provided by Seeking Alpha) below show that earnings are expected to be $1.40 per unit for 2025, and that earnings are expected to grow going forward.

FY

EPS

YoY

PE

Sales

YoY
2025

1.40

+9.66%

12.43

$92.55B

+11.95%
2026

1.59

+13.25%

10.97

$96.74B

+4.52%
2027

1.66

+4.67%

10.48

$99.20B

+2.54%

As for the balance sheet, Energy Transfer has about $453 million in cash and around $60.61 billion in debt. This is a huge debt load and I believe it shows a lot of leverage being used for this business model. This is not the type of balance sheet that I am comfortable with investing a lot of money in. If there is some type of black swan event in the economy, it will always be the companies with the highest debt loads that see the most amount of selling pressure. We even got a recent taste of that dynamic when Energy Transfer went from trading at around $18, and then plunged almost in a straight line in April to about the $14 level. If we had a real sustained downtrend in the economy and/or oil prices, or even just another stock market correction over tariffs, I believe the debt load on the balance sheet will once again spook investors and lead to a selloff that might make some people realize the downside risks may have been greater than they expected.

The Dividend And Share Buybacks

Energy Transfer pays a quarterly distribution of $0.3275 per unit, which totals $1.31 per unit on an annual basis. On a relative basis, this generates an attractive yield of about 7.5%, although I believe it is not as good as it should be when you consider potential risk factors. One of the potential risk factors is the fact that Energy Transfer is paying out almost everything it earns and this not only means there is little room for error if earnings decline, but it also makes it tough for the payout to grow. When you consider the earnings and the distribution, it suggests a payout ratio of about 98%, and for many stocks in other industries, this could be considered to be a red flag. However, higher payout ratios are tolerated in certain industries like this, and also REITs for example, but I still think this is too high and that it increases risks to the payout in a downturn.

Major Concerns And Potential Risk Factors For Investors

As I have already brought up, the extremely high payout ratio is a big concern for me, and so is the very significant debt load. The combination of these two factors are even more concerning and could lead to a major problem for investors if there is some type of major macro challenge or a company specific issue. I always have to wonder that if a business model is so good, then why is it so loaded with debt and where have all the profits been going? When you look at companies that are hugely profitable, they don't have huge debt loads. Just look at the balance sheet of Apple (AAPL) or Alphabet (GOOG), (GOOGL) and you will see an amazing business model, a cash rich balance sheet, and a very low payout ratio.

In 2020, Energy Transfer cut the distribution by 50%, and this shows how a sudden event like a pandemic and oil price drops could create a big problem for income investors. It's worth noting that Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Enbridge (ENB) did not cut their distributions in 2020.

One of the reasons Energy Transfer has lots of debt on the balance sheet is because it has made a number of acquisitions in the past. It could make another acquisition in the future, and this could create downside for investors. There was a very challenging acquisition attempt in the past that cost Energy Transfer a significant sum and also made some investors concerned about management decisions and governance risks. I believe it makes sense to consider these potential risk factors before investing in Energy Transfer. It has made a number of acquisitions over the past few years and has made about one or two transactions each year lately. Investors should consider that additional acquisitions are likely going forward, and that could pose various risks. Here are examples of some recent acquisitions:

In 2024, it acquired WTG Midstream

In 2023, it acquired Crestwood Equity Partners

In 2023, it acquired Lotus Midstream

In 2022, it acquired Woodford Express

Another issue for me is that investors in Energy Transfer will receive a K-1 form. I generally avoid investing in Limited Partnerships because there have been times when the company issuing them sent them late, or sent an amended K-1. I have not had this issue with Energy Transfer, but it is something that I think about when assessing how attractive an investment opportunity might be. Also, K-1's can create tax complications in other ways that might be more than some investors want to deal with. All things being equal, if I am given a choice between investing in a dividend stock that pays a similar yield as an MLP that issues a K-1, I will pick the dividend stock. Just as an example, Verizon (VZ) is currently yielding nearly 7%, and it has a payout ratio of just about 58%. In addition, it is trading for just about 8 times earnings estimates. All of these metrics for Verizon look much better to me, when I compare it to Energy Transfer.

Where I Could Be Wrong

If the Federal Reserve cuts rates significantly, this could create upside for Energy Transfer investors. I do think rates will come down eventually, but it might not be for a positive reason. That's because rate cuts often happen when the economy is heading into a recession. I do like the valuation of Energy Transfer units when it comes to earnings estimates and the suggested price to earnings ratio. This valuation suggests there could be upside for unitholders, perhaps back up to around the 52-week high in the $21 price range.

At What Price Would I Buy Energy Transfer?

As I mentioned earlier, it seems like there is some gravitational pull for Energy Transfer that has recently taken these units back down to the $15 and even $14 range. I would consider accumulating at these levels, but I would not make this a large position in my portfolio.

I would also consider selling a limited amount of put options, which could either generate some potential income or allow me to buy units at lower prices.

In Summary

I don't feel that the current distribution yield of about 7.5% is attractive enough for me given my concerns about the high payout ratio and debt load, as well as potential acquisition and governance risks. The fairly recent past history of a whopping 50% distribution cut is also in the back of my mind, along with the potential tax implications that a K-1 can create. Energy Transfer units would have to trade back to the $14 to $15 range for me to consider buying again, so for now I think it deserves a hold rating. In the meantime, Verizon is looking like a stock I will be accumulating at current levels. Please let me know what you think in the comments below.

No guarantees or representations are made. Hawkinvest is not a registered investment advisor and does not provide specific investment advice. The information is for informational purposes only. You should always consult a financial advisor.

data.title: How Long Will Tariff-Related Uncertainty Last? Business Execs Are Split
data.description:
By Jose Maria Barrero | Nick Bloom | Steven J. Davis | Kevin Foster | Aaron Jalca | Brent Meyer

On July 8, a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs ended. Only to be extended, for the most part, until August 1. We think. Maybe. Don't worry if you're confused; you're not alone. (By the way, here's a quick primer on the current state of flux.)

Tariff-related uncertainty has skyrocketed since February 2025. One often-cited measure that captures the frequency of articles about trade policy in U.S. newspapers reached all-time highs in its 40-year history and remains several times its pre-2025 average.

Given the sizeable effect policy-related uncertainty can have on firms' willingness to move forward on capital investment projects and hire new workers, we posed a set of questions to firms in our Survey of Business Uncertainty in June (our fielding period was June 9–20) to better understand the breadth of tariff-related uncertainty and to gauge how long business execs expect it to last.

Across more than 900 responses to the question of whether respondents' firms were facing "...any uncertainty related to tariffs or trade policy?”, seven out of every 10 executives said yes. While that figure may appear to be high, it touches on the importance of global trade in the US economy and how heavily the issue is weighing on the minds of business decision-makers.

What is, perhaps, unsurprising is that the presence of tariff-related uncertainty is highest for manufacturing firms, a heavily trade-sensitive sector, where nearly 90 percent of execs reported facing tariff-related uncertainty in June. As figure 1 shows, the pervasiveness of tariff-related uncertainty was nearly as elevated in the retail and wholesale trade sector (roughly 85 percent) and in the construction, real estate, and mining sectors (nearly 70 percent). Interestingly, more than half of services firms reported facing tariff-related uncertainty as well. Potential explanations range from concerns over the impact of tariffs on consumer demand for services to worries over retaliatory tariffs. (This explanation especially begs us to field a follow-up question on the specific effect that uncertainty is exerting on these firms!)

While trade policy uncertainty seems to be part of the zeitgeist now, a key question that may likely determine the propensity for this uncertainty to disrupt firms' planning processes and fundamentally change business activity is whether or not it is fleeting. And, here, business execs are of two minds.

Figure 1: Seventy percent of business executives report facing tariff-related uncertainty.

Is your firm currently facing tariff-related uncertainty?

The typical firm (as measured by the median respondent) anticipates tariff-related uncertainty to be largely resolved by November 2025. But measures of central tendency like the mean and median are not very useful in characterizing diffuse (and multi-modal) distributions such as the one shown in figure 2. What we can say is that business executives disagree to a large extent on how long it will take for trade-policy uncertainty to appear in the rearview mirror rather than on the windshield.

Even using simple "eyeball econometrics,” it appears that there are roughly two camps. A narrow majority of firms see trade policy uncertainty settling down by the end of the year. Another, albeit smaller, camp sees uncertainty over the direction of trade policy continuing to be a salient feature of the economic landscape for the better part of the next three years or so.

Figure 2: Firms are split on how long before tariff-related uncertainty will be resolved.

When do you think it is most likely that the trade and tariff-related uncertainty will be resolved?

As events of the past few weeks have made clear, trade policy continues to evolve. For executives in our Survey of Business Uncertainty, uncertainty over tariffs and trade policy remains pervasive. Moreover, beliefs over when this uncertainty will fade vary widely. As of mid-June, a slight majority of firms see a relatively quick end to trade policy uncertainty, but a large cohort expect the fog of uncertainty to persist awhile. We will continue to explore these questions in the Survey of Business Uncertainty, so stay tuned.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vltava Fund Q2 2025 Letter To Shareholders
data.description:
SHIPS ON THE WAVES

When people ask me why I invest in stocks, I tell them that it is because I’m trying to reduce certain risks in my life. Investing in stocks is often associated in the public mind with maximizing returns or with risky speculation. Experience tells me, however, that sophisticated investors think differently. In their eyes, investments serve a much more important function. They see investments as a tool for managing and reducing financial risks, and most especially risks associated with long-term currency depreciation and inflation. Many people tend to understand investing in stocks as something that brings risk rather than something that reduces risk. Yes, investing in stocks, like any other activity, carries with it certain risks. But taking reasonable risks can in return reduce other, much more substantial long-term risks that investors face.

Vltava Fund is a fund for qualified investors. The definition as to who is regarded as a qualified investor changes over time. One of the current conditions is that every such investor must have a certain relatively large amount of assets. Our investors meet this condition, and some of them have truly vast assets. This puts most of them in a somewhat different position compared to ordinary retail investors with regard to why they invest and what they want to achieve. In addition to their desire to realize an attractive return, most of them also strive to protect the value of the assets they already have – not only for themselves but often also for future generations.

If a person has greater wealth and holds it in cash or other low-yielding assets, this may seem safe at first glance, but the long-term, systematic, and essentially inevitable devaluation of the currency will exist as a constant threat. The real value of their savings is very likely to decline significantly over time. It can be said that a person who has greater assets automatically becomes an investor, because even the decision not to invest has a major impact on the real value of his or her assets.

The impact of currency devaluation is very much evident with the passage of time. Over the past 80 years (that is to say in one human lifetime), cumulative inflation in the US has exceeded 1,500% (Source: Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis). Simply put, what 80 years ago cost $10 would cost more than $150 today. The US currency has lost more than 90% of its value during that time. The dollar is the main global reserve currency, and yet its value is rapidly declining. A handful of countries (for example, Switzerland and Japan) are doing slightly better, but most developed countries are even worse off (the UK, Australia, France), and that’s not even to mention the many countries such as Turkey, Russia, Argentina, Brazil, and Israel. The future does not look bright when one considers the rates at which most currencies are depreciating. Indeed, quite the opposite. Structural deficits, growing government debt, and long-term expansionary monetary policies suggest that currency devaluation will continue to be the main risk with which investors must contend. Investing in stocks can help them to do just that. Exactly 80 years ago, the S&P 500 stock index (SP500, SPX) stood at 14.87. As I write these lines, its value is 6,092. That is more than 400 times higher. Taking into account and reinvesting all dividends (which is largely just a theoretical exercise), the total return would be even higher, exceeding 5,000 times. Using the US market in these comparisons amounts to a bit of “data mining” inasmuch as few other markets have similarly high 80-year real returns, but the argument about stock returns compared to the rate of currency depreciation is valid also more generally.

Vltava Fund is an equity fund. Therefore, we do not consider which asset classes to invest in as part of our investment considerations. We see our fund as a product intended for the equity portion of our individual investors’ asset allocations. When selecting individual stocks for the Vltava Fund portfolio, however, we constantly remind ourselves of the long-term goals for most of our investors. We try to put aside what is happening today and instead visualize what may happen in a few years. Rather than to consider a speculative horizon of less than 90 days (3 months), we prefer to consider an investment horizon longer than 1,090 days (3 years). Market developments often tempt us to think in a manner that is too short-term in nature, and we believe that to be a mistake. The past few months have provided a case in point. The market has been hit by dramatic statements that would make it easy to succumb to emotion, to overreact, or to try and time the market.

You may have noticed that our letters to shareholders do not very often dive into politics. It is not our tendency to overestimate the extent to which politics influence the stock markets. Oftentimes, people misjudge in just such a manner. The main economic trends and, above all, significant developments in individual companies span several governments and often defy party labels. If I count correctly, in the 21 years of Vltava Fund’s existence, we have already lived through seven US presidential terms. As for some of the other countries in which we have long-term investments, the UK has had 8 prime ministers in that time, Germany 4 chancellors, Japan 11 prime ministers, and the Czech Republic 9 prime ministers.

When I look back at our own investments, the most common denominator among those that have been most successful was that these were high-quality, well-managed companies whose value climbed markedly over time. The development of their share prices then favorably reflected both the growth in the company’s value and its undervaluation at the time of purchase. In the case of investments that did not work out for us, the most common denominator was that our perception of the company’s quality and value had been mistaken. I cannot recall a single case where we decided to buy or sell an investment based upon who was or was not the president or prime minister in a given country at the time. Politics influence global developments, but in long-term investing, as we understand and practice it at Vltava Fund, we consider it much more important to focus on analyzing individual companies and looking for those that have strong competitive advantages, capable management, strong free cash flow, potential for long-term growth, and effective asset allocation. These considerations, combined with an emphasis on attractive stock valuations, allow us to look beyond the daily news and price charts and to benefit from an advantage that cannot easily be quantified but is nevertheless very valuable — the advantage of patience and a long-term perspective.

The saying “what doesn’t kill you makes you stronger” can be applied to high-quality companies. The world is constantly changing and evolving. The economic, political, regulatory, and competitive environments within which individual companies operate are in a state of continuous change. We look upon companies as living organisms in which people create value through their work and at the same time influence their capability to adapt to changing business conditions. Better companies tend to have greater adaptability and overall resilience, and very often they emerge stronger from the maelstrom of events. Lower quality companies many times face deterioration in their market positions, marginalization, and sometimes even existential challenges. We have seen examples of such development many times in the markets, which is why we at Vltava Fund strive to look beyond the horizon of current events when selecting investments. In valuing companies, we emphasize their abilities to adapt to change, to innovate, and at the same time to remain true to their corporate cultures. We believe that the key to long-term success is not to try to predict every wave that will sweep across the market but to find ships that are built to sail even through storms. If you look at our portfolio, I believe you will agree that it is dominated by companies possessing just these qualities. Such companies allow us to take advantage of something that the market often disregards: time.

Time and a longer perspective also provide for another opportunity that markets offer to long-term investors. The vast majority of both amateur and professional investors and speculators operating in the stock markets have time horizons that are too short. They try to find stocks where they feel there is a chance for quick profits. At the same time, they tend to ignore stocks where the hope of getting rich quickly is small but the probability of attractive long-term returns is high. This activity creates great competition at the short end of the market; but, as the market lengthens, competition diminishes. Taking advantage of this phenomenon is sometimes referred to as time arbitrage. If you look at the individual stocks in the Vltava Fund portfolio, almost all of them offer little hope of getting rich quick but a high probability of attractive long-term returns. It seems to me that, as the years go by, the conditions for taking advantage of time arbitrage are improving more and more, and we are striving to take maximum advantage of this. It makes a lot of sense to us. Such investments do not require taking on a lot of risk.

Changes in the portfolio

We added no new companies to our portfolio this past quarter, nor did we get rid of any. Nevertheless, we were rather active and focused on buying additional shares, especially during April, when the prices of some stocks were so low that we couldn’t even believe our eyes. Usually, that is a good sign. We strengthened our positions in 10 different companies, which represent half of our portfolio. In percentage terms, we increased our holdings most in Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX), United Rentals (URI), and Arrow Electronics (ARW). We also added to our position in the Nikkei 225 index. (NKY:IND)

We welcome the volatility that this year’s events have brought to the markets. The more stock prices fluctuate, the more they deviate from their fundamental values (in both directions) and the more often they give us opportunity to take advantage of this. As a result of our long-term analytical work, we have a list of companies that we like and whose fundamental values we understand. We are continuously expanding and updating this list, inclusive of our assessments of fundamental values. This, then, is a kind of shopping list of stocks, and currently it comprises about 100 companies. Most of these stocks are trading most of the time at prices that are too high for us to buy. We are accustomed to waiting patiently for some of their prices to fall convincingly below the companies’ intrinsic values. Greater market volatility means that this favorable situation arises more frequently. Even during relatively calm times, it is normal for the price of a typical stock to fluctuate within a range of 40% of its initial price during the year. This does not require any dramatic developments within any given company. In contrast, the fundamental value of a company usually changes much more slowly. The volatility of share prices is several times greater than is that in the development of fundamental share values. Our approach to the markets is based on this fact, which has been proven many times over. It is founded on an activity known as “pricing” (meaning to take advantage of a favorable ratio between the share price and a stock’s value) and avoids an activity termed “timing” (meaning to attempt at predicting market movements and trying to profit from them in the short term). Pricing is a much easier and more logical way to invest money in stocks than is timing.

While trying to predict short-term market developments, it is not enough in and of itself for one correctly to estimate what the immediate political, economic, and other developments will look like. It is necessary also to know what other investors are expecting and to what extent this is already reflected in prices. Imagine that you have been invited to a Ball of Illusions. In a luxurious hall full of mirrors and soft whispers, a hundred guests gather to take part in a beauty contest organized by a prominent patron. Six ladies in silk dresses, each beautiful in her own way, stand on the stage. But the winner will not be chosen based upon who the individual spectators like the most. No. The task of each spectator is to choose the lady they think the others will choose as the most beautiful. Sounds strange? That’s the magic of it. This is a Keynesian beauty contest. Economist John Maynard Keynes used it as a metaphor for investor behavior in the markets. When investors buy stocks, often they do not choose the one that might be seen as having the greatest real value but rather the one they think others will think has value. And that’s a game of illusions — just like at the ball.

We avoid short-term timing and playing with illusions. Pricing is at the heart of our considerations. We are aware that estimates of a company’s intrinsic value necessarily embody certain elements of subjectivity, but, by taking a conservative approach to valuation and ensuring that there is a margin of safety between the price and the value of the share, it is possible to invest money in shares calmly and methodically without having to take great risks. In this way, the speculative element in investing is kept to a minimum. We hope that the markets will remain volatile in the future and that we will be able to take advantage of this. This year has brought a few opportunities. For example, when Williams-Sonoma’s (WSM) share price was above $200 at the beginning of the year, it was significantly higher than our estimate of its intrinsic value. We sold the shares. Conversely, when United Rentals’ share price was below $600 in March and April, it was significantly lower than our estimate of its intrinsic value. We have now added it to our portfolio.
